subject: Physics
q: A circular loop of radius 7 cm is placed in uniform magnetic field of 0.2 T directed perpendicular to plane of loop. The loop is converted into a square loop in 0.5 s. The EMF induced in the loop is $\_\_\_\_$ mV.
question: A circular loop of radius 7 cm is placed in uniform magnetic field of 0.2 T directed perpendicular to plane of loop. The loop is converted into a square loop in 0.5 s. The EMF induced in the loop is $\_\_\_\_$ mV.
solution:
Circular loop with radius $r = 0.07$ m in magnetic field $B = 0.2$ T.
Initial flux through circular loop: $\Phi_1 = B \times \pi r^2 = 0.2 \times \pi(0.07)^2 = 0.000980\pi$ Wb
The loop is converted to a square maintaining the same perimeter: $C = 2\pi r = 0.14\pi$ m
Side of square: $a = \frac{0.14\pi}{4} = 0.035\pi$ m
Final flux through square loop: $\Phi_2 = B \times a^2 = 0.2 \times (0.035\pi)^2 = 0.2 \times 0.001225\pi^2$ Wb
Evaluating: $\Phi_1 = 0.2 \times 0.0154 = 0.00308$ Wb
$\Phi_2 = 0.2 \times 0.001225 \times 9.87 = 0.00242$ Wb
Change in flux: $\Delta\Phi = 0.00308 - 0.00242 = 0.00066$ Wb
Average EMF: $\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta\Phi}{\Delta t} = \frac{0.00066}{0.5} = 0.00132$ V $= 1.32$ mV

subject: Physics
q: In a coil of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>, the magnetic flux due to an external magnetic field varies with time as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ϕ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mfenced><mrow><mn>9</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>. The value of total heat produced in the coil, till the flux becomes zero, will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
question: In a coil of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>, the magnetic flux due to an external magnetic field varies with time as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ϕ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mfenced><mrow><mn>9</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>. The value of total heat produced in the coil, till the flux becomes zero, will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
solution:
It is given that magnetic flux ϕ=239-t2

For magnetic flux to be zero 0=239-t2⇒t=3 s

According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction,

ε=-dϕdt=-230-2t=4t3

Heat produced from 0 to 3 s,

H=∫03ε2Rdt=∫0316t29×8dt=2 J

subject: Physics
q: A conducting circular loop is rotated about its diameter at a constant angular speed of $100 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ in a magnetic field of 0.5 T perpendicular to the axis of rotation. When the loop is rotated by $30^{\circ}$ from the horizontal position, the induced EMF is 15.4 mV. The radius of the loop is $\_\_\_\_$ mm. (Take $\pi=\frac{22}{7}$)
question: A conducting circular loop is rotated about its diameter at a constant angular speed of $100 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ in a magnetic field of 0.5 T perpendicular to the axis of rotation. When the loop is rotated by $30^{\circ}$ from the horizontal position, the induced EMF is 15.4 mV. The radius of the loop is $\_\_\_\_$ mm. (Take $\pi=\frac{22}{7}$)
solution:
$E = B\omega A \sin\omega t$
$15.4 \times 10^{-3} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 100 \times \dfrac{22}{7} r^2 \times \dfrac{1}{2}$
$r = \sqrt{\dfrac{15.4 \times 28 \times 10^{-5}}{22}}$
$R = 14 \text{ mm}$

subject: Physics
q: The current in an inductor is given by $\mathrm{I}=(3 \mathrm{t}+8)$ where $\mathrm{t}$ is in second. The magnitude of induced emf produced in the inductor is $12 \mathrm{mV}$. The self-inductance of the inductor _____ $\mathrm{mH}$.
question: The current in an inductor is given by $\mathrm{I}=(3 \mathrm{t}+8)$ where $\mathrm{t}$ is in second. The magnitude of induced emf produced in the inductor is $12 \mathrm{mV}$. The self-inductance of the inductor _____ $\mathrm{mH}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}=3 \mathrm{t}+8 \\ & \varepsilon=12 \mathrm{mV} \\ & |\varepsilon|=\mathrm{L}\left|\frac{\mathrm{dI}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right| \\ & 12=\mathrm{L} \times 3 \\ & \mathrm{~L}=4 \mathrm{mH}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A circular current loop of radius $R$ is placed inside square loop of side length $L$ $(L >> R)$ such that they are co-planar and their centers coincide. The permeability of free space is $\mu_0$. The mutual inductance between circular loop and square loop is _______.
question: A circular current loop of radius $R$ is placed inside square loop of side length $L$ $(L >> R)$ such that they are co-planar and their centers coincide. The permeability of free space is $\mu_0$. The mutual inductance between circular loop and square loop is _______.
solution:
Using the reciprocity theorem, assume a current $I$ flows through the larger square loop and compute the flux through the smaller circular loop. The mutual inductance is given by $M = \dfrac{\Phi}{I}$.

Magnetic field at the centre of the square loop:
For a square of side $L$, the perpendicular distance from the centre to any side is $d = \dfrac{L}{2}$, and each side subtends angles $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 45^\circ$ at the centre.

The field due to one finite straight segment is:
$B_{\text{segment}} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi d}(\sin\theta_1 + \sin\theta_2)$

$B_{\text{segment}} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi \cdot \frac{L}{2}}\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi L} \cdot \sqrt{2} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 I}{2\pi L}$

All four sides contribute equally in the same direction at the centre, so:
$B_{\text{total}} = 4 \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 I}{2\pi L} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 I}{\pi L}$

Flux through the circular loop:
Since $L \gg R$, the field $B_{\text{total}}$ can be treated as uniform over the area $A = \pi R^2$ of the circular loop.

$\Phi = B_{\text{total}} \cdot A = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 I}{\pi L} \cdot \pi R^2 = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 R^2 I}{L}$

Mutual inductance:
$M = \dfrac{\Phi}{I} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 R^2}{L}$

Hence, the correct option is $(4)\ \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\,\mu_0 R^2}{L}$.

subject: Physics
q: An insulated copper wire of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> turns is wrapped around a wooden cylindrical core of the cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The two ends of the wire are connected to a resistor. The total resistance in the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. If an externally applied uniform magnetic field in the core along its axis changes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> in one direction to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> in the opposite direction, the charge flowing through a point in the circuit during the change of magnetic field will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mC</mi></math>.
question: An insulated copper wire of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> turns is wrapped around a wooden cylindrical core of the cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The two ends of the wire are connected to a resistor. The total resistance in the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. If an externally applied uniform magnetic field in the core along its axis changes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> in one direction to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> in the opposite direction, the charge flowing through a point in the circuit during the change of magnetic field will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mC</mi></math>.
solution:
The induced emf ε in the turns of the coil can be written as

ε=-dφdt=iR   ...1

Simplify equation (1) to obtain the required charge through a point in the circuit.

-dφdt=iR⇒-1R∫φ1φ2dφ=∫idt⇒q=φ1-φ2R   ...2

Where, φ1, φ2 are the magnetic flux associated with the coil from two ends.

As, φ=NBA, from equation (2), it can be written that

q=NARB1-B2   ...3

where, N, A are the number of turns and the cross-sectional area of the core respectively.

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required charge.

q=100×24×10-4 m2×1.5 T--1.5 T12 Ω=0.06 C×1000 mC1 C= 60 mC

q: A circular coil of 1000 turns each with area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is rotated about its vertical diameter at the rate of one revolution per second in a uniform horizontal magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>07</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The maximum voltage generation will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
question: A circular coil of 1000 turns each with area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is rotated about its vertical diameter at the rate of one revolution per second in a uniform horizontal magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>07</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The maximum voltage generation will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
solution:
Given, N=1000, A=1 m2 and B=0.07 T

The maximum emf generated is εmax=NBAω,

=NBA2πf

 =1000×0.07×1×2π1=140π

Thus, maximum emf is 439.6 V≈440 V.

q: The total number of turns and cross-section area in a solenoid is fixed. However, its length <math><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> is varied by adjusting the separation between windings. The inductance of solenoid will be proportional to:
question: The total number of turns and cross-section area in a solenoid is fixed. However, its length <math><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> is varied by adjusting the separation between windings. The inductance of solenoid will be proportional to:
solution:
Self-Inductance =μ0n2πr2L (n = number of turns per unit length)
=μ0NL2πr2L
=μ0N2πr2L
Self-Inductance ∝1L

The correct statement about the directions of induced currents I1 and I2 flowing through R1 and R2 respectively is :
question:
A conducting bar of length L is free to slide on two parallel conducting rails as shown in the figure

Two resistors R1 and R2 are connected across the ends of the rails. There is a uniform magnetic field B→ pointing into the page. An external agent pulls the bar to the left at a constant speed v.

The correct statement about the directions of induced currents I1 and I2 flowing through R1 and R2 respectively is :
options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are in anticlockwise direction

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are in clockwise direction

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is in clockwise direction and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is in anticlockwise direction

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is in anticlockwise direction and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is in clockwise direction
solution:
Given, the external agent pulls the bar towards left. According to Lenz's law and Fleming's right hand rule, there will be a motional EMF generated in the bar. The right-hand rule tells us that the magnetic force on positive charges will be in downward direction and the electric equivalent circuit can be drawn as shown below.

So, I1 is in the clockwise direction and I2 is in the anticlockwise direction.

In equilibrium the potential difference between the faces of higher and lower potential developed because of the motion through the field will be mV.

In equilibrium the potential difference between the faces of higher and lower potential developed because of the motion through the field will be mV.
solution:
Using the equation

 qE=qvB ⇒E=vB

Using the relation ∆V=Ed

⇒∆V=vBd

=2×12×0.15 V
=0.15 V=150 mV

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: Self inductance of a long solenoid of length $L$, total number of turns $\mathrm{N}$ and radius $\mathrm{r}$ is less than $\frac{\pi \mu_0 N^2 r^2}{L}$.
Statement 2: The magnetic induction in the solenoid in Statement 1 carrying current $I$ is $\frac{\mu_0 N I}{L}$ in the middle of the solenoid but becomes less as we move towards its ends.
question:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: Self inductance of a long solenoid of length $L$, total number of turns $\mathrm{N}$ and radius $\mathrm{r}$ is less than $\frac{\pi \mu_0 N^2 r^2}{L}$.
Statement 2: The magnetic induction in the solenoid in Statement 1 carrying current $I$ is $\frac{\mu_0 N I}{L}$ in the middle of the solenoid but becomes less as we move towards its ends.
options: Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is the correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not the correct explanation of Statement 1.
solution:
Self inductance of a long solenoid is given by
$$
L=\frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{l}
$$
Magnetic field at the centre of solenoid
$$
B=\frac{\mu_0 N I}{l}
$$
So both the statements are correct and statement 2 is correct explanation of statement 1

solution: At $\mathrm{t}=10 \mathrm{sec}$ complete loop is in magnetic field therefore no change in flux $\mathrm{e}=\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=0$ $\mathrm{e}=0$ for complete loop

subject: Physics
q:
A square loop of side 2.0 cm is placed inside a long solenoid that has 50 turns per centimetre and carries a sinusoidally varying current of amplitude 2.5 A and angular frequency 700 rad s-1. The central axes of the loop and solenoid coincide. The amplitude of the emf induced in the loop is x×10-4 V. The value of x is ___________

( Take, π=227
question:
A square loop of side 2.0 cm is placed inside a long solenoid that has 50 turns per centimetre and carries a sinusoidally varying current of amplitude 2.5 A and angular frequency 700 rad s-1. The central axes of the loop and solenoid coincide. The amplitude of the emf induced in the loop is x×10-4 V. The value of x is ___________

( Take, π=227
solution:
It is given that the current is varying sinusoidally. The current can be written as I=I0sinωt.

The magnetic field through the solenoid B=μ0nI

The flux through the square is ϕ=μ0nIA

The emf is

 ε=μ0nA×d(I0sinωt)dt⇒ε=μ0nAI0ωcosωt

The amplitude of the emf is,

ε=μ0nAI0ω=4π×10-7×5010-2×4×10-4×2.5×700⇒ε=44×10-4 V

q:
A coil is placed in a magnetic field B→ as shown below :

A current is induced in the coil because B→ is :
question:
A coil is placed in a magnetic field B→ as shown below :

A current is induced in the coil because B→ is :
options: parallel to the plane of coil and increasing with time
parallel to the plane of coil and decreasing with time
solution:
Lenz's law state that the direction of the induced current is such that it opposes its cause of generation, so, 


B→, must not be parallel to the plane of coil for non-zero flux and according to Lenz's law if B is outward it should be decreasing for anticlockwise induced current.

q: A 1 m long metal rod XY completes the circuit as shown in figure. The plane of the circuit is perpendicular to the magnetic field of flux density 0.15 T. If the resistance of the circuit is 5 Ω, the force needed to move the rod in direction, as indicated, with a constant speed of 4 m s-1 will be _______10–3 N.
question: A 1 m long metal rod XY completes the circuit as shown in figure. The plane of the circuit is perpendicular to the magnetic field of flux density 0.15 T. If the resistance of the circuit is 5 Ω, the force needed to move the rod in direction, as indicated, with a constant speed of 4 m s-1 will be _______10–3 N.
solution:
The force on the rod is given by

F=ilB

⇒F=BvlR×lB

⇒F=B2l2vR   ...(i)

The data given is

R=5 Ωl=1 mv=4 m s-1B=0.15 T

Substituting the values in equation (i)

F=0.152×12×45

=18×10-3 N

subject: Physics
q: A coil of area A and N turns is rotating with angular velocity \(\omega\) in a uniform magnetic field \(\vec{B}\) about an axis perpendicular to \(\vec{B}\). Magnetic flux \(\varphi\) and induced \(\operatorname{emf} \varepsilon\) across it, at an instant when \(\vec{B}\) is parallel to the plane of coil, are :
question: A coil of area A and N turns is rotating with angular velocity \(\omega\) in a uniform magnetic field \(\vec{B}\) about an axis perpendicular to \(\vec{B}\). Magnetic flux \(\varphi\) and induced \(\operatorname{emf} \varepsilon\) across it, at an instant when \(\vec{B}\) is parallel to the plane of coil, are :
solution: $\begin{aligned} \phi & =\mathrm{BAN} \cdot \cos (\omega \mathrm{t}) \\ \varepsilon & =\frac{-\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{BA} \omega \mathrm{~N} \cdot \sin (\omega \mathrm{t}) \end{aligned}$ When $B$ is parallel to plane, $\underline{\underline{\omega t}}=\frac{\pi}{2}$ $\Rightarrow \phi=0, \varepsilon=\mathrm{BA} \omega \mathrm{~N}$

question: The magnetic flux through a coil perpendicular to its plane is varying according to the relation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ϕ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>5</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> Weber. solution:
Given, ϕ=5t3+4t2+2t-5

Magnitude of induced emf ε=dϕdt=15t2+8t+2

At, t=2 s, ε=60+16+2=78 V

Current, i=εR=785=15.6 A

q:
A circular conducting coil of radius 1 m is being heated by the change of magnetic field B→ passing perpendicular to the plane in which the coil is laid. The resistance of the coil is 2 μΩ. The magnetic field is slowly switched off such that its magnitude changes in time as  

B=4π×10-3 T1-t100

The energy dissipated by the coil before the magnetic field is switched off completely is E=______mJ.
question:
A circular conducting coil of radius 1 m is being heated by the change of magnetic field B→ passing perpendicular to the plane in which the coil is laid. The resistance of the coil is 2 μΩ. The magnetic field is slowly switched off such that its magnitude changes in time as  

B=4π×10-3 T1-t100

The energy dissipated by the coil before the magnetic field is switched off completely is E=______mJ.
solution:
ϕ=B→·S→

ϕ=4π×10-31-t100·πR2

ϕ=4×10-3×121-t100

ε=-dϕdt

ε=-ddt4×10-31-t100

ε=4×10-31100=4×10-5 V

When B=0

1-t100=0

t=100 sec

Heat =ε2Rt

Heat =4×10-522×10-6×100 J

Heat =16×10-10×1002×10-6 J

Heat =0.08 J

Heat =80 mJ

q: A bar magnet is passing through a conducting loop of radius R with velocity v. The radius of the bar magnet is such that it just passes through the loop. The induced e.m.f. in the loop can be represented by the approximate curve:
question: A bar magnet is passing through a conducting loop of radius R with velocity v. The radius of the bar magnet is such that it just passes through the loop. The induced e.m.f. in the loop can be represented by the approximate curve:
solution:
As Magnet is approaching toward the loop magnetic flux increases and Rate of increment of flux also increases so emf increases with time

 at this moment emf is maximum and negative

 Magnet is in middle of coil, at this moment emf is equal to zero

 Now again flux start changing however polarity of emf get reverse

 At this moment emf is again maximum

 emf again become zero

q: In an $\mathrm{AC}$ generator, a coil with $\mathrm{N}$ turns, all of the same area $\mathrm{A}$ and total resistance $\mathrm{R}$, rotates with frequency $\omega$ in a magnetic field $B$. The maximum value of emf generated in the coil is "
question: In an $\mathrm{AC}$ generator, a coil with $\mathrm{N}$ turns, all of the same area $\mathrm{A}$ and total resistance $\mathrm{R}$, rotates with frequency $\omega$ in a magnetic field $B$. The maximum value of emf generated in the coil is "
solution: NBA $\omega$

subject: Physics
q: The arm PQ of a rectangular conductor is moving from x=0 to x=2b outwards and then inwards from x=2b to x=0 as shown in the figure. A uniform magnetic field perpendicular to the plane is acting from x=0 to x=b. Identify the graph showing the variation of different quantities with distance:
question: The arm PQ of a rectangular conductor is moving from x=0 to x=2b outwards and then inwards from x=2b to x=0 as shown in the figure. A uniform magnetic field perpendicular to the plane is acting from x=0 to x=b. Identify the graph showing the variation of different quantities with distance:
solution:
As rod moves in field area increases upto x=b, then field is absent and again flux is generated on return journey from x=b to x=0. Thus plot A for flux.
⇒e=-dϕdt⇒ curve B for emf
⇒ Power dissipated =vi⇒ curve C for power dissipated

q: A small square loop of wire of side l is placed inside a large square loop of wire LL≫l. Both loops are coplanar and their centres coincide at point O as shown in figure. The mutual inductance of the system is
question: A small square loop of wire of side l is placed inside a large square loop of wire LL≫l. Both loops are coplanar and their centres coincide at point O as shown in figure. The mutual inductance of the system is
solution:
Let a current, I pass through the square loop of side, L. The magnetic field at the centre O,

B=4×Magnetic field due to each side

⇒B=4×μ04π⋅IL/2sin 45∘+sin 45∘

⇒B=2μ0IπL12+12=22μ0IπL

Magnetic flux linked with the small square loop,

ϕ=BA=Bl2=22μ0Il2πL

Mutual inductance of the coil, 

M=ϕI=22μ0l2πL.

subject: Physics
q: At time t=0 magnetic field of 1000 Gauss is passing perpendicularly through the area defined by the closed loop shown in the figure. If the magnetic field reduces linearly to 500 Gauss, in the next 5s, then induced EMF in the loop is:
question: At time t=0 magnetic field of 1000 Gauss is passing perpendicularly through the area defined by the closed loop shown in the figure. If the magnetic field reduces linearly to 500 Gauss, in the next 5s, then induced EMF in the loop is:
solution:
ε=-dϕdt=-AdBdt
ε= − Δϕ Δt = AΔB Δt
=16×4-4×21000-5005×10-4×10-4
=56×5005×10-8=56×10-6V

subject: Physics
q:
A circular loop of radius $20\text{ cm}$ and resistance $2\ \Omega$ is placed in a time varying magnetic field $\vec{B} = (2t^2 + 2t + 3)\text{ T}$. At $t = 0$, for the plane of the loop being perpendicular to the magnetic field and, the induced current in the loop at $t = 3\text{ s}$ is $\dfrac{\alpha}{50}\text{ A}$. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
(Take $\pi = 22/7$)
question:
A circular loop of radius $20\text{ cm}$ and resistance $2\ \Omega$ is placed in a time varying magnetic field $\vec{B} = (2t^2 + 2t + 3)\text{ T}$. At $t = 0$, for the plane of the loop being perpendicular to the magnetic field and, the induced current in the loop at $t = 3\text{ s}$ is $\dfrac{\alpha}{50}\text{ A}$. The value of $\alpha$ is _______.
(Take $\pi = 22/7$)
solution:
The magnetic flux linked with the circular loop is given by $\Phi = BA \cos \theta$. Since the plane of the loop is perpendicular to the magnetic field, the angle between the area vector and the magnetic field is $\theta = 0^{\circ}$.

$\Phi = B \cdot \pi r^2$

Given $r = 20\text{ cm} = 0.2\text{ m}$, the area of the loop is:

$A = \pi (0.2)^2 = 0.04\pi\text{ m}^2$

The induced emf in the loop is given by Faraday's law of induction:

$|E| = \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = A \dfrac{dB}{dt}$

Given the magnetic field $B = 2t^2 + 2t + 3$, we differentiate it with respect to time $t$:

$\dfrac{dB}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt}(2t^2 + 2t + 3) = 4t + 2$

At $t = 3\text{ s}$, the rate of change of the magnetic field is:

$\dfrac{dB}{dt} = 4(3) + 2 = 14\text{ T/s}$

Now, substituting the values into the emf equation:

$|E| = 0.04\pi \times 14 = 0.56\pi\text{ V}$

The induced current $I$ in the loop is:

$I = \dfrac{|E|}{R} = \dfrac{0.56\pi}{2} = 0.28\pi\text{ A}$

Using $\pi = \dfrac{22}{7}$, we get:

$I = 0.28 \times \dfrac{22}{7} = 0.04 \times 22 = 0.88\text{ A}$

We are given that the induced current is $\dfrac{\alpha}{50}\text{ A}$. Equating the two values:

$\dfrac{\alpha}{50} = 0.88$

$\alpha = 0.88 \times 50 = 44$

Answer: $44$

subject: Physics
q: An infinitely long straight wire carrying current I, one side opened rectangular loop and a conductor C with a sliding connector are located in the same plane, as shown in the figure. The connector has length l. and resistance R. It slides to the right with a velocity v. The resistance of the conductor and the self inductance of the loop are negligible. The induced current in the loop, as a function of separation r, between the connector and the straight wire is
question: An infinitely long straight wire carrying current I, one side opened rectangular loop and a conductor C with a sliding connector are located in the same plane, as shown in the figure. The connector has length l. and resistance R. It slides to the right with a velocity v. The resistance of the conductor and the self inductance of the loop are negligible. The induced current in the loop, as a function of separation r, between the connector and the straight wire is
solution:
The induced current in the wire  will be due to the motional EMF developed in the moving resistor  and that back emf developed  at that point will be  equal to  VBL

V=velocity of the conductor

B=perpendicular magnetic field

L=length of conductor

We know magnetic field due to a long wire is 

B=μ0i2πr

Hence the induced current across wire will be :

I=eR

I=μ02πIvℓRr

subject: Physics
q: When the current changes from $+2 \mathrm{~A}$ to $-2 \mathrm{~A}$ in $0.05$ second, an e.m.f. of $8 \mathrm{~V}$ is induced in a coil. The coefficient of self-induction of the coil is
question: When the current changes from $+2 \mathrm{~A}$ to $-2 \mathrm{~A}$ in $0.05$ second, an e.m.f. of $8 \mathrm{~V}$ is induced in a coil. The coefficient of self-induction of the coil is
solution: $e=-\mathrm{L} \frac{\mathrm{dl}}{\mathrm{dt}} \Rightarrow 8=(\mathrm{L}) \frac{2-(-2)}{0.05} \Rightarrow \mathrm{L}=0.1 \mathrm{H}$

subject: Physics
q: A rectangular loop of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and width <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is placed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to a magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The loop is removed from the field in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>sec</mi></math>. The average emf induced in the loop during this time is
question: A rectangular loop of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and width <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is placed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to a magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The loop is removed from the field in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>sec</mi></math>. The average emf induced in the loop during this time is
solution:
The formula to calculate the change in the magnetic flux associated with the given loop can be written as

∆ϕ=-BAcosθ=-Blbcosθ   ...1

The formula to calculate the induced emf is given by

ε=-∆ϕ∆t=Blbcosθ∆t   ...2

From equation (2), it follows that

ε=4×2.5×2×cos60°10 V=1 V
